EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a surface trough northwest of the Aloha State, surface winds became light and from the southeast.  This resulted in afternoon and evening cloud buildups over portions of Oahu and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The heavy rain that occurred caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

Heavy downpours occurred over portions of North and South Kohala on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A fire charred 60 acres of brush above a Kaanapali Coffee farm in west Maui.  The blaze affected electric poles and downed power lines but did not damage any structures.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of damages was not available.  The cause of the fire was unknown.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai; the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  Lifeguards helped a 17-year-old male who was seriously injured when he was caught in the shore-break at Waimea Bay on Oahu's North Shore.  Ocean safety personnel also performed 1120 preventive actions, just on North Shore beaches.  No serious property damage was reported.

A brush fire that began Aug. 22nd continued to smolder and occasionally reignite in small areas in west Oahu near Makakilo.  Most of the fire was inaccessible at higher elevations; and firefighters, in the end, had to tackle the blaze on foot using hand tools and water from backpacks.  The fire burned more than 1000 acres, and was caused by 7-year-old twin brothers playing with a lighter.  No serious injuries were reported.  The cost of any damages was not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weliweli and Ala Kinoiki roads closed due to high water.

A front at the surface and its accompanying upper air trough brought heavy downpours and thunderstorms to the islands.  Flash flooding conditions developed on Maui, the Big Island of Hawaii, and Kauai as the system slowly made its way through the Aloha State. High winds and blizzard conditions also occurred at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of significant injuries or property damage, though more than 60 hikers had to be evacuated from Kalalau Valley on the north side of Kauai on December 24th after multiple streams became impassable because of several days of moderate to heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mauna Kea Summit road was closed due to sustained winds over 65 mph.

A cold front moved rapidly through the Aloha State.  There were only limited areas of heavy rain as the system moved from west to east across the island chain, though a flash flood did occur in East Maui.  Also, a male tourist was struck by a falling branch at a golf course on Oahu and died from his injuries.||Power poles and lines, and trees, were downed in several areas as the front pushed through the state; a metal roof was blown off a home in Kunia Camp on Oahu; and three boats ran aground, and a boat lost its moorings, near Hilo on the Big Island.  Very strong winds also occurred over the Big Island summits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Piilani Highway, or Highway 31, was closed between mile marker 31 and Nuu Landing in East Maui due to high water.  This is the Hawelewele Gulch area near Kaupo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male tourist from Japan was hit by a falling tree branch at Leilehua Golf Course in central Oahu and died from his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of swells from powerful lows far northwest of the islands generated surf of 20 to 50 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 15 to 30 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; 15 to 25 feet along the north-facing shores of the Big Island; and 10 to 18 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.  County officials closed many beaches because of the danger posed by such extreme surf.  The surf did contribute to erosion problems, especially on the north shore of Oahu.  Also, many areas near the coasts being affected by the high surf were inundated by water and debris.

A front at the surface and its accompanying upper air trough brought heavy downpours and thunderstorms to the islands.  Flash flooding conditions developed on Maui, the Big Island of Hawaii, and Kauai as the system slowly made its way through the Aloha State. High winds and blizzard conditions also occurred at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of significant injuries or property damage, though more than 60 hikers had to be evacuated from Kalalau Valley on the north side of Kauai on December 24th after multiple streams became impassable because of several days of moderate to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Akoni Pule Highway, Highway 270, closed at mile marker 27 because of high water.

A fire burned 150 acres of brush in the Puu Waawaa forest reserve in the North Kona District on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  Investigators believe the blaze was started by lightning.  There were no serious injuries reported.  The cost of any damages was not available.

Moisture from a dissipating front was the fuel necessary as heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms affected the state as an upper trough moved near the island chain.  Wintry weather and high winds were also a part of the mix at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A fire blackened 25 acres of dry brush near Anahola on the island of Kauai.  The fire came close to several homes, and most residents voluntarily left their homes while firefighters got the blaze under control.  The fire damaged one small storage shed on a lot, and some residents suffered minor irritation from smoke inhalation.  Other than that, no significant property damage or injuries were reported.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

An upper trough near the eastern end of the Aloha State acted on low level moisture to produce heavy downpours, including isolated thunderstorms.  The results were ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Showers moving along on a southeast flow affected parts of East Maui.  The heavy rain persisted for a time over upslope portions of Haleakala and produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding along roadways.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

An upper trough that had been northwest of the Aloha State began moving east.  As it did, the system generated showers and isolated thunderstorms over the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The heavy rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no serious injuries or property damage reported.

An upper trough slowly losing its grip on the Aloha State as it moved east helped to cause one last round of heavy showers over the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain led to small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

The combination of a surface trough moving through the isles and an upper trough triggered heavy rain for the western part of the Aloha State, and flash flooding on the Garden Isle of Kauai.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The costs of any damages were not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kauai Civil Defense reported that Puolo Road in Hanapepe had been closed due to flooding.

A lingering surface trough triggered heavy rain over the Big Island of Hawaii.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Recent rains across the Aloha State were sufficient in most stricken areas to ease problems to below the D2 level of severe drought.  The one area that remains is in West Molokai, which remained at the D3 level of extreme drought through March.

Upper level instability over the Aloha State helped to enhance late afternoon and evening showers in Kona on the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain brought nuisance flooding and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A blaze consumed 270 acres of sugar cane in Paia on the island of Maui.  The cane was owned by the Hawaiian Commercial & Sugar Company, HC&S.||The cause of the fire was under investigation.  There were no significant injuries.  The cost of damages had not been assessed.

A blaze, started by 7-year-old twin brothers playing with a lighter, blackened more than 1000 acres of brush in West Oahu near Makakilo.  The fire forced residents of about 20 homes to evacuate the area for three days, and one home suffered minor damage of a scorched wall and door.  The blaze continued to smolder and occasionally reignite in inaccessible areas through the end of the month.  No serious injuries were reported.

With daytime sea breezes, cloud buildups over some of the isles led to heavy rain and isolated thunderstorms.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

The impacts of Tropical Storm Iselle were mainly sustained over the east and southeast portions of the Big Island of Hawaii, in the Puna and Kau Districts. Although there were no reports of significant injuries or fatalities from Iselle, preliminary reports from the isle listed more than 200 homes and several businesses with at least some damage, and 116 infrastructure problems (including downed utility poles and power lines, and damaged roads).  Agriculture was affected most especially, with around 50% of the state's papaya crop destroyed, at an estimated loss of $55 million.  The storm also caused damage to other crops; including flowers, macadamia nuts, and coffee.  Total costs of damages were not available, but a local private economist said a ballpark estimate for total losses by the state were between $148 million and $325 million.||Additionally, swells from Iselle and Hurricane Julio generated surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island.

As Hurricane Ana moved to the south of the island chain, it brought heavy precipitation  from the Big Island westward to Niihau.  The system also generated isolated thunderstorms as it headed west.||The swell from Ana produced high surf, mainly along south shores of the islands and in the range of 8 to 15 feet.  Overall, there were no reports of significant property damage or injuries from either the rain or the surf.

On the Big Island of Hawaii, a lava flow from Kilauea volcano, which began June 27, ignited a fire that consumed 300 acres of brush in the Puna District just south of the Ainaloa Estates subdivision.  The blaze did not threaten any structures.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

With Hurricane Ana having passed to the west of the islands, a moist and somewhat unstable air mass remained over the Aloha State.  This led to heavy rain over parts of Oahu; especially the Koolau Range, and locales in the eastern half of the isle.  The downpours resulted in ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai; the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 14 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai.  Lifeguards helped a 17-year-old male who was seriously injured when he was caught in the shore-break at Waimea Bay on Oahu's North Shore.  Ocean safety personnel also performed 1120 preventive actions, just on North Shore beaches.  No serious property damage was reported.

As Hurricane Ana moved to the south of the island chain, it brought heavy precipitation  from the Big Island westward to Niihau.  The system also generated isolated thunderstorms as it headed west.||The swell from Ana produced high surf, mainly along south shores of the islands and in the range of 8 to 15 feet.  Overall, there were no reports of significant property damage or injuries from either the rain or the surf.

Abundant moisture in the lower levels of the atmosphere and a weak trough aloft combined to generate heavy showers and thunderstorms around the Aloha State.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.  This is a continuation of the weather pattern that existed over the islands at the beginning of the month.

A swell from a powerful low off the Kamchatka Peninsula generated surf of 20 to 30 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and along the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai, and the north-facing shores of the Big Island; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.  Lifeguards on Oahu rescued 16 individuals from north and west shore beaches, and made more than 3500 warnings and preventative actions during the day on the 11th.  However, no significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A weak cold front moving through the western end of the Aloha State caused heavy rain to fall over the Garden Isle of Kauai.  The rain caused the Hanakapiai Stream in the northwest part of the isle to rise and become impassable.  More than 120 hikers became stranded but were later rescued by fire department personnel.  There were no significant injuries or serious property damage.

A fire charred 1022 acres of brush near Kaaulalu Bay in the Kau District in the southern part of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The blaze began in the afternoon of the 1st, and was finally under control and contained in the afternoon of the 4th.||Persistent trade winds in the initial phase of the fire caused it to spread rapidly, but weather conditions became more favorable, with occasional rain and less wind, later on the 1st, and then through the 4th.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.  The cause of the fire was under investigation.

Moisture from an old front and heating during daylight hours combined to produce heavy rain over the Big Island of Hawaii and Maui.  During one rain event, a mudslide occurred along the Hana Highway in East Maui.  Otherwise, the precipitation caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred along the Hana Highway one mile west of Keanae at 5:40 PM.  The road was closed in the area for several hours as crews worked to open one lane by midnight.  The road was completely reopened at 4:30 AM the next day, May 3rd, after most of the earth and debris had been removed.

An upper trough near the Hawaiian Islands acted on remnant moisture from former Tropical Storm Wali to generate heavy showers and thunderstorms.  The rain was intense enough to produce flash flooding in windward parts of Oahu and in windward West Maui.  Strong winds accompanied the precipitation, and blew down trees and damaged homes.  Also, a man, snorkeling with a group, died when he succumbed to strong winds and high waves that battered the area off Maui on the 20th.||The cost of damages to homes and other structures, and other physical property, was not available.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding in Kahakuloa Stream inundated the bridge in Kahakuloa town in windward West Maui and briefly closed Kahekili Highway in that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The impacts of Tropical Storm Iselle were mainly sustained over the east and southeast portions of the Big Island of Hawaii, in the Puna and Kau Districts. Although there were no reports of significant injuries or fatalities from Iselle, preliminary reports from the isle listed more than 200 homes and several businesses with at least some damage, and 116 infrastructure problems (including downed utility poles and power lines, and damaged roads).  Agriculture was affected most especially, with around 50% of the state's papaya crop destroyed, at an estimated loss of $55 million.  The storm also caused damage to other crops; including flowers, macadamia nuts, and coffee.  Total costs of damages were not available, but a local private economist said a ballpark estimate for total losses by the state were between $148 million and $325 million.||Additionally, swells from Iselle and Hurricane Julio generated surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusted over 50 knots and damaged four roll-up doors at the recently built state airport fire station.  There was also damage to a maintenance facility.

A surface trough passing to the south of the Aloha State, with moisture from former Tropical Storm Fausto, helped trigger heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms over the Big Island of Hawaii and Maui.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

An upper trough that had been northwest of the Aloha State began moving east.  As it did, the system generated showers and isolated thunderstorms over the lee side of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The heavy rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no serious injuries or property damage reported.

Windy conditions, heavy showers, and high surf from a trade wind swell were all part of the panoply of weather that affected the Hawaiian Islands in the middle of March.  The strong winds toppled trees and power lines, and blew the roofs off several homes throughout the state.  Heavy rain caused ponding and isolated debris flows on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  The trade wind swell produced surf of 8 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  No serious injuries were reported.  The costs of damages across the island chain were not available.

An area of moisture from an old frontal boundary caught up in the trade wind flow produced heavy showers over parts of Maui and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported, though several hikers were stranded for a time, in the period from 21 to 24 March, in Waipio Valley on the Big Island because of persistent rainfall in the area.  The hikers were eventually rescued by fire department personnel.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of Hawaii generated surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  Officials on Kauai closed beaches for a time because of the high surf.  Although lifeguards were busy making occasional rescues, giving out warnings, and taking preventive actions; there were no reports of significant injuries or property damage.

With daytime sea breezes, cloud buildups over some of the isles led to heavy rain and isolated thunderstorms.  The precipitation produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

A surface trough moved to a position just west of the Aloha State and brought heavy rain to Kauai and Oahu.  The precipitation caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Abundant moisture in the lower levels of the atmosphere and a weak trough aloft combined to generate heavy showers and thunderstorms around the Aloha State.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

An upper trough near the Hawaiian Islands acted on remnant moisture from former Tropical Storm Wali to generate heavy showers and thunderstorms.  The rain was intense enough to produce flash flooding in windward parts of Oahu and in windward West Maui.  Strong winds accompanied the precipitation, and blew down trees and damaged homes.  Also, a man, snorkeling with a group, died when he succumbed to strong winds and high waves that battered the area off Maui on the 20th.||The cost of damages to homes and other structures, and other physical property, was not available.

A cold front moved rapidly through the Aloha State.  There were only limited areas of heavy rain as the system moved from west to east across the island chain, though a flash flood did occur in East Maui.  Also, a male tourist was struck by a falling branch at a golf course on Oahu and died from his injuries.||Power poles and lines, and trees, were downed in several areas as the front pushed through the state; a metal roof was blown off a home in Kunia Camp on Oahu; and three boats ran aground, and a boat lost its moorings, near Hilo on the Big Island.  Very strong winds also occurred over the Big Island summits.

Heavy rain, including a thunderstorm, affected windward parts of the Big Island of Hawaii near Hilo.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Moisture from a dissipating front was the fuel necessary as heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms affected the state as an upper trough moved near the island chain.  Wintry weather and high winds were also a part of the mix at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

With a surface trough northwest of the Aloha State, surface winds became light and from the southeast.  This resulted in afternoon and evening cloud buildups over portions of Oahu and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The heavy rain that occurred caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.

Moisture from an old front and an upper low south of the Aloha State combined to generate heavy downpours and isolated thunderstorms, from Kauai to the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.
